title: The AIADMK Government That Deceives Persons with Disabilities!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy prepared for this translation; the frozen source chapter remains unchanged and authoritative. The page images confirm the opening address `உடன்பிறப்பே,`, which OCR omitted, and the phrase `தலைமைச் செயலகத்தில்`, which OCR damaged. Clearly mechanical spacing and page-break errors have also been repaired without modernising or rewriting the source. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: As early as 2007, the United Nations discussed the practice of referring to people who are deaf, blind, unable to speak, unable to use their arms or legs, or living with mental illness as “differently abled” rather than “disabled.” The reasoning was that when the functioning of one organ is impaired, another ability in them becomes stronger and more distinctive.
Following that discussion, the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities was established, and India became the seventh country to sign it.
That Convention became international human rights law on 3 May 2008.
Accordingly, I announced that in Tamil Nadu, those who had until then been called “disabled persons” would henceforth be called “persons with different abilities.”

Thereafter, in the 2010-2011 Budget presented on 19 March 2010, the first Budget in the new Legislative Assembly, it was announced that “a separate department for persons with disabilities will function under the direct supervision of the Chief Minister.” A government order was issued that very day.
Following this, a new “Department for the Welfare of the Differently Abled” was created at the Secretariat on 27 March 2010, and an IAS officer assumed charge as its Government Secretary.
As a continuation of this measure, the DMK government issued an order on 27 March 2010 changing the names of the institutions and offices functioning under the Tamil Nadu administration:
The “Office of the State Commissioner for the Disabled” became the “Commissionerate for the Welfare of the Differently Abled.”
The “Tamil Nadu Welfare Board for the Disabled” became the “Tamil Nadu Welfare Board for the Differently Abled.”
The “State Commissioner for the Disabled” became the “Commissioner for the Differently Abled.”
The “District Rehabilitation Officer for the Disabled” became the “District Differently Abled Welfare Officer.”
These changes were put into effect.

For the first time in 2010-2011, a separate demand for grants for the Department for the Welfare of the Differently Abled was presented in the Assembly. This created an opportunity to examine their welfare in detail and formulate new schemes.

Under the previous AIADMK government, Rs. 49 crore had been allocated for the welfare of persons with disabilities in 2005-2006. The DMK government progressively increased that allocation, raising it three-and-a-half times to Rs. 176 crore in 2010-2011.

The Welfare Board for the Differently Abled, headed by the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu, was reconstituted under the DMK government on 26 April 2010. Persons with disabilities above the age of ten were registered as members of the Board. During the DMK government’s tenure, 1,99,856 persons with disabilities joined the Welfare Board.

I donated the Rs. 45 lakh I received for writing the story and dialogue of the film *Ilaignan* to the Chief Minister’s Public Relief Fund. That amount was subsequently given in 2010-2011 to the Tamil Nadu Welfare Board for the Differently Abled for its social-security schemes.

Members of the Association of Blind College Students and Graduates undertook a hunger strike for three days, pressing nine demands. They were denied even permission to meet the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu and explain their grievances, and 200 persons with disabilities were arrested.

Speaking about this, Nagarajan, president of the Association of Blind Graduates and College Students, accused the AIADMK government: “Every year, the government says that it spends several crores of rupees for the welfare of persons with disabilities. But apart from distributing folding sticks, spectacles and similar items, it has implemented nothing else.” This report appeared in *Dinamalar* on 19 September 2013.

But do you know how the government approached persons with disabilities when they protested under the DMK administration? One morning, I read in the newspapers that they were conducting a hunger strike at Chennai’s Rajiv Gandhi Government General Hospital. Without even eating breakfast, I came downstairs, got into the car and went straight to the site of their protest. I told them their demands would be accepted immediately and brought the protest to an end.

The AIADMK government repeatedly refused even to meet persons with disabilities. Only after they protested did a few announcements emerge in the Chief Minister’s name. One announcement said: “To enable unemployed B.Ed. graduates with disabilities to find work more easily, the Teachers Recruitment Board will conduct a separate Special Teacher Eligibility Test. B.Ed. graduates who qualify in this examination will be appointed to existing backlog vacancies and to vacancies arising in future.” Yet *The Hindu Tamil* reported on 24 November 2013 that persons with disabilities were disappointed because even the government order for this had not been issued.

It was also during this government’s tenure that news emerged about Selvaraj, a person with a disability who had travelled from Chennimalai in Erode district, hoping to meet the present Chief Minister Jayalalithaa and obtain financial assistance. Security officials stopped him, and he died by suicide.
Against the scale of what we achieved for persons with disabilities under the DMK government, what is their condition today under AIADMK rule? Here is a report published today, 1 September 2014:

“The Association for All Types of Differently Abled and Protection of Rights has alleged that rules hostile to persons with disabilities are being enforced harshly in Tamil Nadu. Jhansi Rani, president of the Tamil Nadu Association for All Types of Differently Abled and Protection of Rights, said that in 2011 the Tamil Nadu government announced that there were 22 lakh persons with disabilities of all categories in the state. But under the AIADMK government in 2014, that number has been reduced to 16 lakh.

“Yet no census, statistical details or documents have been released. The government has neither explained the basis on which the number fell nor given reasons for the reduction. Tamil Nadu has more than 30 lakh persons with disabilities. Despite demands made over many years, the government has not come forward to provide a maintenance allowance of Rs. 1,000. In Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Kerala and other states, a person with 40 per cent disability is issued a disability certificate and receives financial assistance and concessions. In Tamil Nadu, however, a person is recognised as disabled and becomes eligible for welfare assistance only with a disability of 60 per cent or more. In addition, the family’s property must not exceed Rs. 5,000; the household must not have a male member over the age of eighteen; and annual income must be below Rs. 24,000. Rules hostile to persons with disabilities are being enforced harshly here,” Jhansi Rani said.

From this, we can understand the many ways in which persons with disabilities are treated under this government. Today’s report further states that sugar, wheat and other ration commodities are not provided to them here; that hostile rules are enforced harshly; and that the three per cent reservation for persons with disabilities in government employment has not been announced transparently and must instead be secured through the courts. She has expressed anguish over all this. The report also says that the government acts unilaterally without holding discussions with representatives of persons with disabilities and that not one issue has so far been resolved.

The report says that there is an income ceiling for receiving concessions. But in the Budget read in the Assembly by Finance Minister Professor Anbazhagan during the DMK government in 2010-2011, page 42, paragraph 89, we announced: “The income ceiling prescribed for persons with disabilities to receive assistance under the State Government’s various welfare schemes will be removed entirely. The DMK government has already exempted persons with disabilities from paying tuition fees to pursue higher education; from the coming academic year, it will also exempt them from paying special fees.” Against this, fixing an annual income ceiling of Rs. 24,000 now will become a grave setback for persons with disabilities.
That same Budget presented under the DMK government stated:

“Because the DMK government provides a monthly assistance of Rs. 500, without an income ceiling, to persons with intellectual disabilities who cannot care for themselves, 50,000 people are benefiting. Various assistance payments for blind persons and others with disabilities have been doubled. Special employment opportunities have been provided for them to work as teachers. Schools that identify deafness in early childhood and provide special education have been opened in every district. Along with travel concessions for persons with limb disabilities, various assistive devices, including motorised three-wheelers, have been provided for the first time in the country. The unemployment-relief scheme for blind persons has been extended to all categories of persons with disabilities, benefiting 60,000 people. For the first time in India, since 2008-2009, a monthly assistance of Rs. 500 has been provided to 1,000 persons affected by muscular dystrophy. The amount spent on the welfare of persons with disabilities, which stood at Rs. 49 crore in 2005-2006, has been raised to Rs. 176 crore for the coming financial year; and a separate department for persons with disabilities will function under my direct supervision.”

That is what was written in the DMK government’s Budget. From these details, one can understand the degree of protection persons with disabilities received under the DMK government and the extent of the suffering they face now.

The Jayalalithaa government has no one willing to lend an ear to the grievances of the deaf; to open their eyes and look when blind persons come to express their needs; to speak aloud about the lives of those unable to speak; or to extend a helping hand to those unable to use their arms and legs. In short, the AIADMK government has no heart to show compassion toward persons with disabilities!
With affection,
M.K.
2 September 2014
